On 4 March 2025, students, journalists, and citizens gathered in front of the local office of Radio Television of Serbia (RTS) in Nis to call for press freedom and protest against the President who publicly insulted the journalist of this TV station for her reporting on student-led demonstrations.

On 4 March 2025, several hundred citizens, university and high school students protested outside the building of the National Assembly building in Belgrade - Stari Grad, demanding accountability for the loss of 15 lives following the collapse of a concrete canopy at the Novi Sad railway station. As part of the 'Serbia, Stop' campaign, the protesters held a moment of silence lasting 15 minutes to honor the victims and raise awareness of their demands. Later on, some demonstrators threw eggs at the parliament building while chanting, 'Thieves, thieves!'. In other parts of Belgrade, including Novi Beograd and Zemun, citizens also gathered to observe 15 minutes of silence, as part of the 'Serbia, Stop' campaign.

On 4 March 2025, opposition members, including representatives from the Green-Left Front, NPS, SRCE, and SSP, engaged in a physical confrontation with representatives of the ruling coalition, which included representatives from SNS and SPS, as well as with members of the Assembly's security, during a session of the Serbian Parliament in Belgrade - Stari Grad. Opposition members threw smoke bombs, tear gas, and eggs, activated a fire extinguisher, and set off torches. The assembly hall quickly filled with smoke. Amid the turmoil, two female MPs from the ruling SNS party suffered serious injuries, with one experiencing a medical emergency due to pregnancy complications. The confrontation stemmed from the opposition's accusation that the current government had lost its legitimacy to vote on new laws after being dismantled and should only function as a technical government. Furthermore, they demanded that the session focus solely on changes to the Law on Higher Education, which is part of the students' demands who have been occupying their faculties for months. Outside the parliament, citizens gathered as part of the 'Serbia, Stop' campaign, throwing eggs at the building and chanting slogans against the government (coded separately).

Other: Around 4 March 2025 (as reported), in Managua, Managua, the National Assembly of Nicaragua approved a new Electoral Law, proposed by the Government of Ortega-Murillo. The new Electoral Law incorporates the main elements of the previously approved Constitutional Reform. It establishes the figure of co-presidents, extends the term of Government from five to six years, reduces the number of electoral magistrates from ten to six and grants the Presidency the power to appoint the President and Vice-President of the Electoral Body. The Law will also exclude from the political elections all the people who have been declared by the regime as 'traitors to the homeland'. It also establishes the possibility of cancelling political parties based on a citizen complaint and allows active military personnel to become members of the Electoral Body, a measure that was prohibited before. Critics to the Government argued that the new electoral system is a further step towards the establishment of a single-party system and reinforces the power of the Ortega-Murillo regime.

On 5 March 2025, a group of high school students began a protest march from Zrenjanin to Becej, where a larger high school protest was scheduled for March 7. This protest was organized to stand against the pressures faced by striking teachers who are supporting student-led demonstrations and their demands for accountability regarding the loss of 15 lives in the collapse of a concrete canopy at the Novi Sad railway station. Before starting their journey, the students gathered in the city's main square with teachers, fellow students, and local citizens. They held a moment of silence for 15 minutes to honor the victims of the Novi Sad tragedy as part of the 'Serbia, Stop' campaign. On the first day, they walked 26 kilometers and reached Kumane by nightfall, before continuing their journey to Becej the following day.

Russian activists from Queer Madrid, the Feminist Anti-War Resistance, the Free Russians (Rusos Libres) association, and the Free Russia Foundation participated in the demonstration to denounce reproductive pressure on Russian women amid the country's invasion of Ukraine, the gradual ban on abortions, the persecution of trans women and queer people in Russia, and the situation of Russian female political prisoners.
